Issue / failure mode: Dynamic tanks are accidentally retained in the Yang implementation.
Likely cause: Tank-based interpretation survives in case builders, ledgers, or helper defaults.
Consequence: The model again has shared inventory outside the beds, defeating the simplified direct-coupling architecture.
Diagnostic test: Search code/config for Yang-path tank state creation and run a no-tank smoke case.
Clean pass criteria: Yang wrapper creates no dynamic tank inventory for internal transfers; only bed states persist physically.

Issue / failure mode: Wrapper becomes a global four-bed RHS/DAE solve.
Likely cause: The schedule wrapper is mistaken for a process-network simulator.
Consequence: Complexity rises sharply and the implementation diverges from existing toPSAil behavior.
Diagnostic test: Review call graph for any combined [A,B,C,D] numerical state assembly.
Clean pass criteria: No four-bed RHS exists; the wrapper orchestrates temporary two-bed/single-bed calls only.

Issue / failure mode: Core adsorber physics is modified unnecessarily.
Likely cause: Four-bed scheduling work leaks into mass/energy/momentum model code.
Consequence: Existing two-bed validation becomes unreliable and regression risk increases.
Diagnostic test: Diff audit: core adsorber model files should be untouched except documented interface hooks.
Clean pass criteria: Existing two-bed examples remain unchanged; Yang additions are wrapper-level.

Issue / failure mode: Yang schedule manifest is transcribed incorrectly.
Likely cause: The source cycle table has staggered bed roles and mixed/merged durations, so manual transcription is fragile.
Consequence: Wrong bed enters the wrong operation or pair at the wrong time.
Diagnostic test: Generate a normalized manifest from the source table and compare against a human-reviewed Gantt.
Clean pass criteria: Each bed executes the Yang ten-step sequence once per cycle with documented duration handling.

Issue / failure mode: Slot grouping is inferred by row order instead of explicit operation metadata.
Likely cause: A/B/C/D table order is treated as a proxy for physical coupling.
Consequence: Crossed or non-adjacent pairings are miswired without obvious failure.
Diagnostic test: Use a test manifest where the intended pair is not adjacent in row order.
Clean pass criteria: Pairing depends only on explicit pair metadata and operation labels, never row order.

Issue / failure mode: AD&PP is confused with ordinary AD or PP.
Likely cause: The literature label combines adsorption, external product, and provide-pressurization semantics.
Consequence: Backfill gas and product gas are counted or routed incorrectly.
Diagnostic test: Static translation test for all Yang labels, with AD&PP represented as a compound operation.
Clean pass criteria: AD&PP exposes both external adsorption/product behavior and internal BF donor behavior.

Issue / failure mode: Merged duration cells in Yang Table 2 are not normalized before execution.
Likely cause: Source table presentation is copied literally rather than converted into executable operation groups.
Consequence: Some direct transfers appear unmatched or time-shifted.
Diagnostic test: Manifest validator expands the source schedule into atomic executable groups with start/end times.
Clean pass criteria: All direct transfers have a counterpart with compatible time interval and duration basis.

Issue / failure mode: Wrong direct-transfer partners are selected.
Likely cause: Pair map omitted, incomplete, or overwritten by native two-bed assumptions.
Consequence: Gas is transferred between physically wrong beds; CSS and metrics become meaningless.
Diagnostic test: Pair-map validator for EQI, EQII, PP/PU, and AD&PP/BF across all slots.
Clean pass criteria: Every direct-transfer operation has exactly one intended companion bed and operation.

Issue / failure mode: Donor and receiver direction are inverted.
Likely cause: Operation labels are treated symmetrically when Yang semantics are directional.
Consequence: Pressure equalization, purge, or backfill runs backwards.
Diagnostic test: Initialize a donor at higher pressure/composition and check signed transfer direction.
Clean pass criteria: Transfer goes from BD/PP/AD&PP donor role to PR/PU/BF receiver role as specified.

Issue / failure mode: EQI and EQII are collapsed into one generic equalization ledger category.
Likely cause: Native machinery may use a generic paired-bed equalization concept.
Consequence: Pressure-ladder diagnostics cannot distinguish first and second equalization stages.
Diagnostic test: Ledger requires stage labels even when the solver call is generic.
Clean pass criteria: Reports separately show EQI-BD/EQI-PR and EQII-BD/EQII-PR quantities.

Issue / failure mode: PP->PU endpoint mapping is wrong.
Likely cause: Provide purge is a cocurrent depressurization from donor product end, while purge is countercurrent in the receiving bed.
Consequence: Regeneration profiles and waste accounting are distorted.
Diagnostic test: Endpoint audit: PP donor product end feeds PU receiver product end; receiver waste leaves feed end.
Clean pass criteria: Endpoint and waste/product labels match Yang step descriptions.

Issue / failure mode: Terminal states are written back to the wrong named beds.
Likely cause: Temporary two-bed case uses local bed 1/2 indices that are not mapped back to A/B/C/D correctly.
Consequence: Bed histories are permuted and CSS diagnostics become invalid.
Diagnostic test: Round-trip test with distinguishable A/B/C/D initial states through a crossed pair call.
Clean pass criteria: Returned terminal state for each local slot is written only to its original named bed.

Issue / failure mode: Non-participating bed states are overwritten during a slot.
Likely cause: Wrapper resets or copies all four states after each temporary call.
Consequence: Beds that should be idle/external-only inherit unrelated states.
Diagnostic test: Run a slot with one paired call and one idle/external bed with sentinel states.
Clean pass criteria: Only beds participating in a call are replaced; untouched states are bitwise or tolerance-identical.

Issue / failure mode: Initial phase offsets for A/B/C/D are wrong.
Likely cause: All four persistent states are initialized identically or without Yang schedule offsets.
Consequence: Long startup, impossible first slot, or wrong CSS trajectory.
Diagnostic test: Manifest-driven initialization map and first-cycle pressure-history audit.
Clean pass criteria: Initial roles and pressure classes match the chosen Yang starting slot or are explicitly documented.

Issue / failure mode: Temporary two-bed case leaks state or parameters across calls.
Likely cause: Case builder reuses mutable structures without isolating selected bed states.
Consequence: Later operation groups inherit stale boundary conditions or bed identity.
Diagnostic test: Run back-to-back unrelated pairs with sentinel parameters and states.
Clean pass criteria: Each temporary case contains only selected states and intended operation metadata.

Issue / failure mode: Existing toPSAil call receives unsupported Yang labels.
Likely cause: Literature labels are passed directly into native step parsers.
Consequence: Simulation fails late or silently falls back to wrong boundary behavior.
Diagnostic test: Translation coverage test for AD, AD&PP, EQI-BD, PP, EQII-BD, BD, PU, EQII-PR, EQI-PR, BF.
Clean pass criteria: No Yang label reaches core machinery without an explicit native representation and metadata.

Issue / failure mode: Local bed indices obscure global bed identity in logs and outputs.
Likely cause: Temporary cases use A-1/A-2 style labels without wrapper metadata.
Consequence: Debugging and ledger reconciliation become ambiguous.
Diagnostic test: Output/log audit after a crossed pair and a single-bed operation.
Clean pass criteria: Every log/ledger row carries global bed label, local index, operation label, and slot ID.

Issue / failure mode: Internal direct transfers are counted as external product.
Likely cause: Product, purge, equalization, and backfill streams are aggregated without accounting categories.
Consequence: H2 recovery and purity comparison to Yang becomes invalid.
Diagnostic test: Independent component ledger with external feed/product/waste and internal transfer categories.
Clean pass criteria: Internal transfer streams close conservation but do not increase external product totals.

Issue / failure mode: AD&PP/BF split is mishandled.
Likely cause: A donor bed simultaneously produces external product and supplies BF gas to a companion bed.
Consequence: External product flow is overcounted or BF receives the wrong composition.
Diagnostic test: Synthetic AD&PP/BF test with known product split and known receiver state.
Clean pass criteria: BF internal use and external product are separately reported and component-balanced.

Issue / failure mode: Slot-level component balance does not close.
Likely cause: Multiple sequential temporary calls are accumulated without a common accounting convention.
Consequence: Numerical or bookkeeping errors hide behind aggregate cycle metrics.
Diagnostic test: For every slot, compare feed input, external product, waste, internal transfer, and bed inventory change by component.
Clean pass criteria: Per-slot and per-cycle balances close within tolerance; residuals are reported.

Issue / failure mode: CSS is checked on only one bed or one local temporary case.
Likely cause: Existing two-bed-era convergence logic is reused without all-bed aggregation.
Consequence: Simulation stops before A/B/C/D have all converged.
Diagnostic test: Compute per-bed and overall residuals across state_A/state_B/state_C/state_D at cycle boundary.
Clean pass criteria: Release stops only when all-bed residual criteria pass or a maximum-cycle limit is reported.

Issue / failure mode: Thermal CSS converges slower than composition CSS.
Likely cause: Yang-style non-isothermal beds can retain slow wall/temperature dynamics.
Consequence: Cycle appears converged on purity while thermal states are still drifting.
Diagnostic test: Track c, q, gas temperature, and wall temperature residuals separately for all beds.
Clean pass criteria: CSS report identifies which state family controls convergence.

Issue / failure mode: Layered activated carbon/zeolite bed support is assumed rather than verified.
Likely cause: Yang uses layered beds; toPSAil configuration may require special axial material handling.
Consequence: Schedule may be correct but Yang reproduction remains physically mismatched.
Diagnostic test: Audit parameter structures for layer-dependent adsorbent/isotherm assignment.
Clean pass criteria: Layered-bed support is confirmed or the pilot is labeled as a homogeneous surrogate.

Issue / failure mode: Yang model assumptions do not exactly match toPSAil assumptions.
Likely cause: Yang includes non-isothermal, non-adiabatic dynamics with momentum balance and axial dispersion; toPSAil CIS formulation has its own discretization assumptions.
Consequence: Disagreement with Yang performance may be blamed on scheduling when it is model mismatch.
Diagnostic test: Model comparison register for dispersion, pressure drop, heat transfer, isotherms, kinetics, and layers.
Clean pass criteria: Known mismatches are documented before any validation claim.
Source / code anchor: Yang model section; toPSAil model section

Issue / failure mode: Fixed-duration direct coupling misses intended pressure endpoints.
Likely cause: Existing valve or flow settings are reused from tank interpretation.
Consequence: Equalization/backfill stages terminate at implausible pressure classes.
Diagnostic test: Pressure ladder audit after every operation group.
Clean pass criteria: Pressures follow the chosen Yang pressure classes or deviations are documented.

Issue / failure mode: Flow reversals during direct coupling are not logged.
Likely cause: Paired calls can expose edge cases in pressure-driven or flow-driven modes.
Consequence: Conservation residuals or solver failures are hard to diagnose.
Diagnostic test: Flow-sign audit for EQI, EQII, PP/PU, and AD&PP/BF toy cases.
Clean pass criteria: Reversals are finite, interpretable, and balance residuals remain within tolerance.

Issue / failure mode: Event control is added before the fixed-duration wrapper works.
Likely cause: Event objectives across multiple named beds can conflict.
Consequence: Debugging shifts from architecture errors to event-policy ambiguity.
Diagnostic test: Stage gate forbids event-driven Yang runs before fixed-duration direct-coupling tests pass.
Clean pass criteria: Initial release uses fixed durations; events are a later refinement with explicit precedence policy.

Issue / failure mode: Excel/GUI paths remain two-bed oriented.
Likely cause: Published UI was simplified around two adsorbers even if code internals support more.
Consequence: Four-bed tests become brittle or platform-dependent.
Diagnostic test: Build programmatic inputs for wrapper tests and avoid GUI dependence.
Clean pass criteria: A four-bed wrapper test can be constructed and run from code without macro/GUI assumptions.

Issue / failure mode: No direct-coupling regression tests protect the rewrite.
Likely cause: Architecture simplification removes one class of complexity but adds mapping/writeback risk.
Consequence: Future edits reintroduce tanks, wrong pairings, or metric double-counting.
Diagnostic test: Automated test suite covering manifest, pair map, state writeback, conservation, CSS, and two-bed regressions.
Clean pass criteria: All smoke tests run before any Yang performance comparison.

Issue / failure mode: Toy cases pass but Yang failure is not localized.
Likely cause: A single full Yang pilot is attempted without diagnostic staging.
Consequence: Failure root cause is ambiguous: manifest, pairing, physics, numerics, or metrics.
Diagnostic test: Use staged validation: static, inert, direct-pair toy cases, duplicated two-bed cases, then Yang skeleton.
Clean pass criteria: No later gate starts until previous gate has recorded pass/fail evidence.

Issue / failure mode: Result assumptions are not visible at output time.
Likely cause: No-tank, no-global-RHS, fixed-duration, metric-basis, and model-mismatch assumptions are left implicit.
Consequence: Later comparisons appear stronger than the model warrants.
Diagnostic test: Run-output checklist checks for architecture and metric-basis metadata.
Clean pass criteria: Every run artifact states wrapper mode, direct-pair map version, event policy, holdup assumption, and metric basis.

Issue / failure mode: Parallel agents produce incompatible implementations.
Likely cause: Manifest, pair map, state container, case builder, and ledger schemas are not shared.
Consequence: Individually plausible pieces fail to compose.
Diagnostic test: Interface contract review before implementation.
Clean pass criteria: All work packages exchange typed schemas and handoff tests before coding begins.
